_decision.title: proof-requirement benchmark: 12-task corpus — re-uses B4-v5 set plus 6 new proof-sensitive additions
_decision.rationale: B4-v5 tasks (crc32c, utf8-codec, base32-rfc4648, lru-ttl-cache, semver-range, ring-buffer) are carried forward because (a) the B4-v5 Opus-built corpus registry already has candidate atoms for them, and (b) their adversarial traps make hedging vs. substitution easy to observe. Six new tasks are added that are explicitly proof-relevant: crc32c is also a good 'required' candidate (safety-critical checksum), blake3/base64-encode/hmac-sha256 target atoms the operator should seed with L3 proofs for a live run, and json-schema-validate/url-parse-strict are per_block demonstrations. Each task records expected_proof_status to communicate what seeding the operator must do before the paid run.

note: Task corpus for proof-requirement benchmark (wi-1089). Tasks that overlap with B4-v5 reuse existing corpus atoms. New tasks require operator seeding (see METHODOLOGY.md §Prerequisite Seeding).

tasks.seeding_rationale: Checksum correctness is verifiable by test-vector property proofs; L3 accepted proof here makes 'required' mode non-trivially testable.
tasks.adversarial_framing: Polynomial trap: wrong polynomial 0xEDB88320 passes unit tests written with the wrong polynomial. Test vector 0xE3069283 for '123456789' is the canonical discriminator.

tasks.description: UTF-8 encoder/decoder — no TextEncoder/TextDecoder. 1/2/3/4-byte sequences, surrogate pairs, overlong rejection.
tasks.adversarial_framing: API constraint trap: Haiku uses forbidden TextEncoder/TextDecoder. Multi-byte masks wrong on encode; surrogate pair handling wrong on decode.

tasks.description: RFC 4648 Base32 encode/decode — alphabet A-Z2-7. Padding, case-insensitive decode, length validation.
tasks.adversarial_framing: Alphabet trap: Haiku confuses RFC 4648 Base32 (A-Z2-7) with Base32Hex (0-9A-V). Test vector 'MY======' for 'f' is the immediate discriminator.

tasks.description: LRU cache with per-entry TTL. Lazy expiry on get/has/size; capacity counts live entries only.
tasks.adversarial_framing: Interaction trap: stale entries returned on get(), expired entries count toward capacity, ttlMs=0 vs Infinity confusion, wrong LRU order after re-set.

tasks.description: SemVer range satisfaction: ^, ~, >=, <=, >, <, =, *, ||, space-AND. Critical: ^0.x.y semantics.
tasks.adversarial_framing: Caret trap: Haiku applies ^1.x.y semantics to all ranges. ^0.2.3 → correct <0.3.0, Haiku produces <1.0.0.

tasks.description: Fixed-capacity ring buffer: push (eviction return), shift, peek, get (negative index), Symbol.iterator.
tasks.adversarial_framing: Wrap-around trap: dynamic array instead of fixed pre-allocation, head/tail pointer arithmetic wrong after wrap.

tasks.description: RFC 4648 §4 Base64 encode/decode — URL-safe variant (+ → - / → _). Strict padding. No native atob/btoa.
tasks.seeding_rationale: Base64 encode/decode is a prime L3 proof candidate (well-specified RFC, no moving parts). Seeding an L3-accepted proof here lets the benchmark measure preferred/required mode differences for a well-understood codec.
tasks.adversarial_framing: URL-safe alphabet trap: Haiku uses standard Base64 (+,/) not URL-safe (-,_). Also omits strict padding rejection.

tasks.description: BLAKE3 hash of a Uint8Array to 32-byte digest. Pure-JS implementation, no WASM.
tasks.seeding_rationale: BLAKE3 is complex enough that an L3 proof would be materially valuable (an LLM-authored BLAKE3 has a non-trivial error rate on compression function details). This task demonstrates the 'required' mode most forcefully when the atom has a confirmed proof.
tasks.adversarial_framing: Compression-function trap: BLAKE3's G function and chunk-domain separation differ from BLAKE2. Haiku frequently uses BLAKE2 constants.

tasks.description: HMAC-SHA256: raw SHA-256 core + HMAC wrapper. No crypto module. Accepts key and message as Uint8Array.
tasks.seeding_rationale: HMAC is security-critical: a subtle key-pad XOR error produces wrong output that passes most random-vector tests. An L3 proof (with known test vectors) would be the highest-value seeding target in this corpus. The 'required' mode is most meaningful here.
tasks.adversarial_framing: IPAD/OPAD trap: Haiku uses 0x36/0x5C inverted, or forgets to zero-pad short keys to the block boundary (64 bytes for SHA-256).

tasks.description: RFC 3986 URL parse: scheme, userinfo, host, port, path, query, fragment. Rejects malformed input (no silent fixup).
tasks.adversarial_framing: Silent fixup trap: Haiku uses URL constructor (which normalizes invalid inputs) or WHATWG parsing (different from RFC 3986 on edge cases like empty authority).

tasks.description: JSON Schema Draft-07 validator: type, required, properties, additionalProperties, enum, minimum, maximum, minLength, maxLength, pattern.
tasks.adversarial_framing: additionalProperties trap: Haiku allows additional properties when the schema says false. Also omits allOf/oneOf/anyOf (out of scope here, but a common distraction that bloats the implementation).

tasks.description: Compound: SHA-256 hash a payload (Uint8Array → bytes), then Base64url-encode the digest. Designed for per_block mode measurement.
tasks.seeding_rationale: This task is the canonical per_block example: sha256 hash (proof_requirement: 'required' for the hash dimension) + base64url encode (proof_requirement: 'preferred' for the codec dimension). The per_block mode is exercised here exclusively.
tasks.adversarial_framing: Composition trap: Haiku often handles the full compound in one function. The interesting measurement is whether per_block mode nudges the agent to resolve the two sub-intents separately and select proven atoms for the hash sub-intent.
